comments: Session since confirmed as Cozy Powell's Hammer.
File A was discovered on the end of a tape of material from John Peel shows.
File C missing Tommy's end of show comments.

sessions.details: one and only session. Recorded 1981-05-13. No known commercial release.
one and only session (rpt). Recorded 1974-10-15, first TX 29 October 1974 (John Peel, Top Gear). No known commercial release.

track_listing.track: 'Siam (LP-Nick Mason's Fictitious Sports)' (Harvest) cuts out before end

track_listing.details: LP-My Life In The Bush Of Ghosts

transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. This is TV on the Radio here, Thomas the Vance, and welcome once again to The Friday Rock Show.
Tonight, a rather rare session that was recorded back in 1974. It's a set fronted by Cozy Powell, who was backed by Bernie Marsden and Don Airy, plus others.
Also, we have the debut radio session by the three-piece band called Vargas.
On top of that, we've got some classics by Trespass, by Genesis, by Steve Hillage, and The Doors, among others.
And some new releases by Rainbow, by Graham Bonnet, by Kraftwerk, by Sean Tyler, and Wishbone Ash.
Not forgetting our Friday night connection.
But this evening, we start with two new singles.
The song has been kicking around a long time, but it's the new single by Graham Bonnet.
Its title is Liar. It was once a hit for Argent, also in America.
A massive success for a group called Three Dog Night.
Written by Russ Barlard.
Good single for Crayon Bonnet.
Prior to that, the new single by the Tigers of Pantang.
That's called Don't Stop By.
Ten minutes after ten, I'd like to say good luck to a gentleman who's doing his O-level exams very shortly.
That's a gentleman by the name of David Viney.
He's in Cardiff.
Catherine Morel, or Morel possibly, in Bath says hi and good luck.
Roxy, at the moment, is in Montreux in Switzerland.
She wants to be remembered to Sue and Stew, I think it is.
Sue and Stew, or Stew and Sue.
And also to her husband, Fred, and to her delightful son, Dominic Sebastian, who she loves very much indeed.
Somebody put a bet on that I wouldn't mention Mr. and Mrs. Spud of Egham.
Looks like somebody's won the bet because they've just done it.
Hello from Mr. P.A. Taylor, 32 Denham Road, Egham in Surrey, TW2 9BY.
This is the first of four by Vardis.
That was trespass from the album Metal for Mothers came out last year.
The track you just heard was called Storm Child.
Most especially for a gentleman by the name of David Castleman, who lives in Leicester,
who wants to be remembered to Andy and Dave and Tracy and Dave and Rob and Debbie and Mandy,
and everybody else who knows him.
Especially a lady by the name of Patricia.
Down at the old Hammersmith Odeon I was last night to catch a bit of Billy Squire.
Didn't see anything of Whitesnake, but going again Saturday night here.
Last night's performance by Whitesnake was absolutely ace.
While I was up there in the bar, which I always gravitate to for a quick bottle of the old brown stuff,
I met some bikers, Pat and Rory and Ian and Mark and Jez and Sally and Donna
They all come from Ringwood, they listen to the show and I hope they're listening tonight
And I hope they did enjoy Whitesnake last night
Also met some people who wanted me to say hello to everybody at the Northern, down there in Brighton
And before trespass he heard Vardis and they said let's go
And we will, with Genesis
That's a single that's been out for a couple of weeks I do believe
It's by a band called Giddy's Axe
It's a single on ACS Records.
Its title is The Return of the Gods.
Played that for a lady who sent me a postcard,
which indicates on the front the application of the Harbour Bosch process.
Whatever that may mean.
But it's a blooming great lump of heavy metal, and that's what she likes.
She also said thanks for the programme, and I say thanks to Alison Hume,
who lives in Harlow in Essex, who wants to be remembered to everybody who hangs out in Bishop's Staunton.
Prior to that, the live version of Robbery, Assault, and Battery
from the 1977 albums, Seconds Out by Genesis.
I heard the other day that Phil Collins is back in the studio with Genesis,
or was a couple of weeks ago.
That's good news.
Parking back to the Hammersmith Odeon last night,
I met Rail and Mark,
and all the hungry odors I think he's written on this strange joker
out of a pack of cards.
But anyway, all those people like Genesis.
So indeed does Julian Hicks.
He lives at Asheville House, Main Road, right in on Tyne, Tyne and Weir.
He wanted particularly robbery, assault, and battery.
10.31, out of the archives, circa 1974.
Here's Cozy Powell and Friends and Hold On.
It's a track from an album called My Life in the Bush of Ghosts
by Brian Eno and David Byrne.
The track you heard there was called Regiment.
And the vocal on it was actually done by Dunya Yusin,
who was a Lebanese mountain singer.
And the album is brilliant.
It's on PG Records.
It's been out for some time.
Try and get it.
Right, and that cozy pile from our 1974 session,
the track you heard there was called Hold On.
It's a Jan Hammer composition.
And I'll give you the line-up of the cozy pile set later
because there's three more to come.
That was Steve Hillage from the album that came out in 76 called L,
his version of Hurdy Gurdy Man.
Thanks to Stephen Haynes, who lives in Mould in Fluid,
who sent me a load of information about the gong session
that we put out some weeks ago.
A lot of information, in fact, four pages of it.
And in return, I'll say hello to his mates,
to some of the Steve Heritage fans
and the electric gypsies in mould, as he describes them.
His brother, that's the way he spells it, Andy,
and Nick Bird and Nick C and Peter Co-op Coley
and everybody else that knows Stephen Haynes.
Thanks, Steve.
Adele Tarbuck, how are you?
At 11 Highlands Drive, Burgess Hill in West Sussex.
Wanted hurdy-gurdy, ma'am?
And got it?
Prior to that, you heard something from our session by Vardis.
The track was Love is Dead.
But trust it isn't.
Jenny Wilcock, a 49 Station Road chinner in Oxford,
met three guys at Stamford Bridge on a Sunday afternoon.
One of them, she knows, as Barry.
He was a roadie, but the other two, she just doesn't remember their names.
They were last seen wearing hats, splattered with gillen,
or rather chatted with gillen, or nattered with gillen for ages, it says here.
Ian, of course, that is.
And the last scene by the set Jenny Moorcock at Earl's Court Underground.
Well, Jenny, good luck in your search for the fellow called Barry.
Because he's obviously the one you fancy.
Right, it's seven and a half minutes to eleven.
This is the new single by Rainbow.
A bit of magic out of the archives, second 1974.
The melody was written by that brilliant Brazilian gentleman called Ymir Diodato.
But it was played by Cozy Powell and friends.
And the friends were, incidentally, Bernie Marsden on guitar, Don Airy on the keyboards,
It's Clive Chapman doing some amazingly good bass player.
No singer on that particular track, but Frank Aiello does reappear in the program
in some more of the archive stuff that we've got.
And, of course, Cozy's on the drums.
It was called Super Sprut.
And before that, the new single by Rainbow,
which is a remixed version of Can't Happen Here,
a track that was on the last album by Rainbow.
Here's some Rainbow information there.
The tour of the United Kingdom starts on July the 10th at Inglestown up there in Scotland.
and it finishes, according to my list here, in Manchester.
It's time for the Friday Night Connection.
The tracks we played last week were as follows.
Quatermass, from the album Quatermass, the track was called Gemini.
A track called Apollo, by Rush, from Hemispheres.
A track from the latest album by the Moody Blues, the track was called The Voice,
the album Long Distance Voyager.
Gemini, Apollo, Voyager.
American Space Missions, got it?
some people did the winner is paul rushton a 38 yoxall avenue hearts hill stoke-on-trent in

very crafty little friday night connection you're really gonna have to think about this one because

W1A4WW

Sorry we can't afford any more, we're skint just like you are

Their number 21 album in our country at the moment
The album called Computer World
That was the title track
Out on EMI, I love the name of their publishing company
It's called Ling Kling Klang Music.
Pretty soon we'll be able to see them over in our country
because they're going to be doing a tour here shortly.
I think at the moment they're in Spain or somewhere like that.
Then they're going to do Germany and the rest of the continent
and then come and really do us over.
That was especially for William Angus Devaney
of 25 Hampton Crescent, Gravesend in Kent,
and for his mates as well, Stephen, Steve and Steve,
and James and Rob and Paul and Andrew and Gerard
and Chris and Nick and Catherine and Sue and Caroline.
not forgetting Colin Leighton of Walthamstow.
Richard Clouto of Stafford says,
do us a favour, give us a mention,
and say hello to a lady by the name of Piglet,
and tell Piglet it may be anti-feminist, but I really do care.
Also wants me to say hello in another letter of his,
which I've got in front of me,
to everybody who's at the Rose and Crown.
And before that, you heard Vardis, they were steaming along.
This is Sean Tyler from a new album
called Sean Tyler's Just Popped Up.
I think undeniably the best album they've put out in a long, long time.
The new album by Wishbone Ash is out on MCA in a few weeks.
It's called Number the Brave, and that track there was called Open Road.
And I must say, I'm looking forward to seeing them on Tuesday the 2nd of June.
That's when I'm going to sit in the audience there.
And before that, something from a new album called Sean Tyler's Just Popped Out by Sean Tyler,
ex of the Tyler Gang, remember them?
The track was called Diamond Lane, and that album's out on Zilch Records.
It's 23 and a half minutes before midnight on the Friday Rock Show.
And now here's two from our archive session by Cozy Powell and friends.
First, Foolish Girl, and then Keep Your Distance.
Ordered on Tuesday, the 29th of October, 1974.
It was called Keep Your Distance.
Before that, you heard Foolish Girl on the session,
where Cozy Powell on the drums.
Bernie Marsden, now of Whitesnake on guitar.
Don Airy, now of Rainbow on keyboards.
Clive Chapman, I don't know anything about him at all,
playing the bass magnificently.
And the lead vocalist was a man by the name of Frank Aiello,
who at one time was in a band called Bedlam,
along with Cozy Powell, to the best of my knowledge,
and with the bit of research we did this afternoon,
they only had one album out ever in 1973,
and I think it was on Christmas.
Hello and love from Heather to Mick
and to all Michael Schenker fans everywhere.
Must mention Booze of the Bullet,
Kathy, it's you, Rich, Dobber,
